seoTitle: How to Calculate Cycle Time in Jira
metaDescription: Learn how to measure cycle time in Jira to see how long issues actually take from start to finish.
intro: Cycle time tells you how long work actually takes once someone starts on it, which is a key signal for spotting bottlenecks. This guide covers how to calculate and view cycle time using Jira's built-in tools.

steps.heading: Define your start and end statuses
steps.body: Decide which status marks the beginning of work (like 'In Progress') and which marks completion (like 'Done').

steps.heading: Open the Control Chart report
steps.body: On a Kanban board, go to Reports and select the Control Chart, which shows cycle time for completed issues.

steps.heading: Set the date range and status scope
steps.body: Adjust the report to cover the date range and specific statuses you defined as your cycle time boundaries.

steps.heading: Read the average and outliers
steps.body: Review the chart's average cycle time along with any unusually long-running issues that skew the numbers.

steps.heading: Use the data to spot bottlenecks
steps.body: Compare cycle times across issue types or components to find where work consistently slows down.

faq.question: What's the difference between cycle time and lead time?
faq.answer: Cycle time measures from when work actually starts to completion, while lead time measures from creation to completion, including wait time.

faq.question: Does Jira calculate cycle time automatically?
faq.answer: The Control Chart report on Kanban boards calculates it automatically once you define the relevant status columns.

faq.question: Can I calculate cycle time for Scrum projects too?
faq.answer: Yes, though the Control Chart is most commonly associated with Kanban boards; Scrum teams can still apply the same concept manually.

faq.question: Why does one outlier issue skew my average cycle time?
faq.answer: A single issue stuck in progress for an unusually long time can pull the average up significantly, which is why reviewing outliers matters.
